opening_hook.title: The Indiana State Fair Tragedy
opening_hook.text: August 13, 2011. Just minutes before Sugarland was scheduled to perform, high winds caused the stage rigging to collapse into the crowd. Seven people died, and more than 100 were injured. The investigation revealed gaps in weather monitoring, structural inspection, and emergency response planning. This tragedy sparked the creation of the Event Safety Alliance and transformed how the industry approaches safety.
opening_hook.key_lesson: Safety planning isn't just paperwork—it saves lives.

core_concept.title: Life Safety First: Core Principles
core_concept.text: Event safety planning is the systematic approach to identifying hazards, assessing risks, and implementing controls to protect everyone involved in a live entertainment event.

key_principles.description: Protection of human life is the highest priority in all safety decisions
Proactive hazard identification and mitigation prevent incidents
Organizers, contractors, venues, and authorities all have safety roles
Every event is an opportunity to learn and improve safety systems

four_cs_framework.title: The Four C's of Safety Management

components.description: Ensuring all personnel have necessary training, experience, and expertise
Establishing clear authority and enforcing safety policies
Active involvement of all parties in monitoring and improvement
Ensuring everyone understands safety objectives and procedures

content.title: Event Safety Planning Timeline Tool
content.description: Navigate through the complete event lifecycle and identify critical safety activities at each phase.

items.task: Confirm event date with public service agencies
items.rationale: Never promote before confirming agency resource availability

items.task: Identify lead agency and primary contact

items.rationale: Understand liability for injuries, acts/omissions, emergency costs

items.task: Assess community impact and resource requirements

branching_scenario.title: Scenario: Date Selection Conflict
branching_scenario.setup: You want to schedule a major music festival for July 4th weekend. The venue is available, and tickets are selling well.
branching_scenario.decision_point: The fire department informs you they have limited staffing due to a scheduled training exercise and multiple other events that weekend.

options.text: Proceed as planned—hire private security to supplement
options.feedback: Private security cannot replace fire department capabilities. Emergency services must have adequate resources to respond effectively.

options.text: Reschedule the event to a date with full agency support
options.feedback: Correct! The Event Safety Guide recommends not promoting before confirming date/time with public service agencies.

options.text: Reduce event capacity to match available resources
options.feedback: Reducing capacity helps, but may not address all emergency response needs. Consult with agencies on minimum requirements.

items.task: Create health and safety policy statement

timeline_phases.name: Load-In Phase
timeline_phases.description: Equipment delivery, installation, and safety checks

items.task: Post site safety rules at all entrances
Verify first aid facilities available from work start
Mark all fire aisles and egress routes
Maintain communication with all safety personnel
Verify emergency routes clear (check every 30 min)

timeline_phases.name: Load-Out Phase
timeline_phases.warning: Load-out can be particularly dangerous due to haste and fatigue

items.task: Ensure adequate lighting for all work areas

content.title: Virtual Venue Hazard Assessment
content.description: Walk through a virtual event venue and identify potential hazards before they become incidents.

scenario_setup.title: You're the Safety Coordinator
scenario_setup.context: You're conducting a pre-event hazard assessment for a 5,000-person outdoor music festival. Walk through the venue and identify hazards in each area.

hazards.description: Narrow entry gate with fence pinch point
hazards.mitigation: Widen gate to minimum 20ft, add secondary entry point
Mark with paint, add lighting, temporary ramp if needed

interactive_element.instructions: Click on areas where you see potential hazards

hazards.description: High density expected at stage barrier
hazards.mitigation: Install engineered stage barrier, plan for 1 steward per 100 patrons in pit
Verify certified rigger installation, engineer-stamped drawings, daily inspection
Licensed electrician installation, GFCI protection, weatherproofing, clear access
Clear exit immediately, establish 36in minimum width, post exit signs

content.title: Stakeholder Coordination Scenario
content.description: Navigate the complex relationships between event organizers, venues, contractors, and public agencies.

scenario.title: The Multi-Stakeholder Challenge
scenario.setup: You're organizing a 10,000-person outdoor electronic music event. Multiple stakeholders must coordinate effectively for safety.

start.prompt: During the initial planning meeting, the venue manager states they have an existing permit that covers all events. The fire department asks for a site-specific fire safety plan. What do you do?

options.text: Rely on the existing venue permit—it's always been sufficient before
Create a site-specific fire safety plan while reviewing venue permit conditions
Ask the venue manager to handle the fire department requirements

venue_permit_issue.feedback: INCORRECT: Existing permits have specific conditions. Each event requires its own safety assessment. The fire department's request for a site-specific plan is appropriate and necessary.
venue_permit_issue.consequence: Fire department flags your event for additional inspection. Event approval is delayed.

delegation_issue.feedback: INCORRECT: While venue management should be involved, ultimate safety responsibility rests with the event organizer. You cannot delegate your legal obligations.
delegation_issue.consequence: Confusion about who is responsible for fire safety. Potential gaps in emergency planning.

proper_planning.prompt: Good! Now you're selecting contractors. The lowest bidder for stage construction doesn't have certified riggers on staff. They promise to 'figure it out.' What do you do?

options.text: Hire them but require they bring in certified riggers as subcontractors
Select a different contractor with proper certifications and experience
Accept their bid with a requirement for engineer stamps on all drawings

compromise_solution.feedback: PARTIAL: While better than nothing, this adds coordination complexity and potential liability questions. Document everything carefully.

insufficient_controls.feedback: INCORRECT: Engineer stamps on drawings are necessary but not sufficient. Installation requires qualified personnel. Structural failures have caused multiple fatalities in our industry.
insufficient_controls.consequence: Potential structural safety issues. You're assuming significant liability.

safety_priority.prompt: Excellent safety-first thinking! During final preparation, the weather forecast shows potential for severe thunderstorms on event day. The show is sold out. What is your decision?

options.text: Proceed with enhanced weather monitoring and evacuation plan ready
Postpone the event—weather is unpredictable and not worth the risk
Proceed as planned—the forecast might change and fans are expecting the show

weather_success.feedback: CORRECT: Enhanced monitoring with contingency plans is appropriate. You have clear evacuation triggers and shelter identified. This balances safety with event viability.
weather_success.outcome: SUCCESS: Your comprehensive planning results in a safe, successful event. The weather holds, but you're prepared if it doesn't.

overly_cautious.feedback: OVERLY CAUTIOUS: While safety is paramount, postponing at the first sign of weather may be unnecessary. Enhanced monitoring with clear triggers is often sufficient.
overly_cautious.outcome: Event postponed unnecessarily. Significant financial impact and disappointed fans.

weather_danger.feedback: DANGEROUS: Weather forecasting has improved significantly. Ignoring severe weather warnings has led to multiple tragedies including stage collapses and lightning strikes.
weather_danger.outcome: CRITICAL FAILURE: Severe weather hits during event. Your lack of preparation puts thousands at risk.

questions.question: According to the Event Safety Guide, when should an organizer confirm the event date with public service agencies?
options: Before promoting or going on sale with the event
questions.rationale: Section 2.1.4 states organizers should not promote or go on sale before confirming date/time with community public service agencies.

questions.question: Which of the following are part of the Four C's of Safety Management? (Select all that apply)
questions.rationale: The Four C's are Competence, Control, Cooperation, and Communication.

questions.question: Outsourcing event execution relieves organizers of their legal obligations for safety.
questions.rationale: Section 2.3.6 states that even when outsourcing, organizers do not relieve themselves of legal obligations.
